name: JavaScript/TypeScript Bug Patterns
description: Common bug patterns for JavaScript and TypeScript codebases

patterns.message: Accessing property of potentially null or undefined value without null check

documentation.description: Accessing properties on null or undefined values causes runtime TypeError exceptions.
documentation.rationale: Using optional chaining (?.) provides safe property access that returns undefined instead of throwing.

examples.explanation: If user or profile is null/undefined, the bad version throws, while the good version returns undefined.

patterns.message: Promise rejection not handled - add .catch() or use try/catch with await

documentation.description: Unhandled promise rejections can cause silent failures and eventually crash Node.js processes.
documentation.rationale: All promises should have error handling to prevent unhandled rejections.

examples.explanation: Network failures, API errors, or other async issues will be properly caught.

patterns.message: Missing await on async function call - promise not being awaited

documentation.description: Calling an async function without await returns a Promise instead of the resolved value.
documentation.rationale: Forgetting await is a common source of bugs where code appears to work but operates on Promise objects instead of values.

examples.explanation: Without await, data is a Promise, not the user object, causing data.name to be undefined.

patterns.message: Missing null/undefined check before property access or method call

documentation.description: Accessing properties without checking if the object exists can cause TypeError.
documentation.rationale: Defensive programming requires validating data before use, especially with external inputs.

examples.explanation: The function should handle cases where user might be null or undefined.

patterns.message: Unsafe type assertion may cause runtime errors - consider type narrowing instead

documentation.description: Type assertions (as Type) tell TypeScript to trust you, bypassing type checking. If wrong, this causes runtime errors.
documentation.rationale: Prefer type guards or narrowing over assertions. Use assertions only when you have external knowledge TypeScript cannot infer.

examples.bad:
const user = data as User;
console.log(user.email);
examples.explanation: Type guards provide runtime validation, catching mismatches before they cause errors.

patterns.message: Parameter has implicit 'any' type - add explicit type annotation

documentation.description: Parameters without type annotations default to 'any', disabling type checking for that value.
documentation.rationale: Explicit types catch errors at compile time and improve code documentation.

examples.good: function process(data: InputData): string { return data.value; }
examples.explanation: With explicit types, TypeScript catches if data doesn't have a value property.

patterns.message: Promise created but not awaited, returned, or stored - possible fire-and-forget bug

documentation.description: A promise that is not awaited, returned, or stored may indicate a missing await or unintentional fire-and-forget.
documentation.rationale: Floating promises can cause race conditions, unhandled rejections, and unpredictable behavior.

examples.explanation: Without await, 'Done' logs before the database update completes.

patterns.message: Unnecessary 'return await' - just return the promise directly

documentation.description: Using 'return await' is redundant since an async function already wraps the return value in a Promise.
documentation.rationale: Removing unnecessary await improves performance slightly. Exception: keep await in try/catch blocks.

examples.explanation: The extra await adds an unnecessary microtask queue entry.

patterns.message: Async function as Promise executor - errors won't reject the promise

documentation.description: Using an async function as a Promise executor can cause rejections to be swallowed.
documentation.rationale: Errors thrown in an async executor become unhandled promise rejections instead of rejecting the outer promise.

examples.explanation: If fetch throws, the Promise constructor won't catch it properly.

patterns.message: Use optional chaining (?.) instead of && chains for property access

documentation.description: The && chain pattern for null-safe access is verbose and can be replaced with optional chaining.
documentation.rationale: Optional chaining is cleaner, shorter, and conveys intent more clearly.

examples.explanation: Optional chaining is more readable and produces identical behavior.

patterns.message: Use nullish coalescing (??) instead of || for default values

documentation.description: Using || for defaults treats all falsy values (0, '', false) as missing, which may not be intended.
documentation.rationale: The ?? operator only falls back for null/undefined, preserving valid falsy values.

examples.bad: const count = input || 10;  // count is 10 if input is 0
examples.good: const count = input ?? 10;  // count is 0 if input is 0
examples.explanation: With ||, input=0 returns 10. With ??, input=0 returns 0 (likely intended).

patterns.message: Using == instead of === may cause unexpected type coercion

documentation.description: The == operator performs type coercion before comparison, leading to unexpected results.
documentation.rationale: Strict equality (===) compares both value and type without coercion.

examples.explanation: With ==, both 0 and '0' would match. With ===, only the string '0' matches.

patterns.message: Array method callback should return a value

documentation.description: Array methods like map, filter, find expect callbacks to return values. Missing returns cause undefined results.
documentation.rationale: Ensure array method callbacks explicitly return values.

examples.explanation: Without return, map produces an array of undefined values.

patterns.message: Sequential await in loop - consider Promise.all() for parallel execution

documentation.description: Using await inside a loop executes operations sequentially, which can be much slower than parallel execution.
documentation.rationale: When loop iterations are independent, use Promise.all() for parallel execution.

examples.bad: for (const url of urls) { const data = await fetch(url); }
examples.explanation: Parallel fetching is significantly faster when requests are independent.

patterns.message: Promise passed to function expecting void - likely missing await

documentation.description: Passing async functions to methods that expect void callbacks (forEach, event handlers) ignores the returned promise.
documentation.rationale: The promise is discarded, so errors are unhandled and ordering is unpredictable.

examples.explanation: forEach doesn't await the callbacks, so all saves run concurrently with no error handling.

patterns.message: Unsafe member access on value of type 'any'

documentation.description: Accessing members on 'any' typed values bypasses type checking, hiding potential runtime errors.
documentation.rationale: Prefer proper typing or type guards to ensure safe member access.

examples.explanation: With proper types, TypeScript catches if items doesn't exist or isn't an array.

patterns.message: Unnecessary condition - value is always truthy/falsy based on type

documentation.description: Checking truthiness of a value that TypeScript knows is never null/undefined is redundant.
documentation.rationale: Unnecessary conditions add noise and may indicate a misunderstanding of the types.

examples.explanation: Since name is typed as string (not string | null), it's always truthy (except empty string).

patterns.message: Use array literal [] instead of Array constructor

documentation.description: The Array constructor has confusing behavior - Array(3) creates sparse array, not [3].
documentation.rationale: Array literals are clearer and avoid the single-argument confusion.

examples.explanation: Array literals are cleaner and have no surprising edge cases.

examples.bad: const items = new Array(3);  // Creates [undefined, undefined, undefined]
examples.explanation: Single number argument creates sparse array, not single-element array.
